resume:
Product Manager | Growth · Monetization · Platform · 0-to-1 · Data-Driven · Agile · Cross-Functional

Results-oriented, data-driven Product Manager with a proven track record of delivering customer-obsessed, outcome-based roadmaps. Adept at stakeholder management, OKRs, experimentation, A/B testing, funnel optimization, North-Star metrics, PLG, GTM, and full product lifecycle ownership. Strong in user research, JTBD, prioritization frameworks (RICE, MoSCoW), and agile delivery (Scrum, Kanban).

Experience
NimbusPay — Product Manager, Growth (2021-present)
- Drove growth initiatives across activation, retention, and monetization funnels.
- Owned the onboarding experience and led cross-functional squads to deliver impactful outcomes.
- Leveraged data-driven insights and rapid experimentation to optimize key conversion metrics.
- Partnered with design, engineering, marketing, and analytics stakeholders end-to-end.

Stacklane — Associate Product Manager (2019-2021)
- Supported roadmap planning, backlog grooming, and sprint ceremonies.
- Synthesized customer feedback into actionable, high-impact product requirements.

Education
BS, Information Systems.

Skills
SQL, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Figma, Jira, A/B testing, SQL, roadmapping, OKRs, PLG, GTM, experimentation.
jd: We're looking for a mid-level Product Manager to own activation and onboarding for our B2B fintech product. You'll be accountable for a specific North-Star input metric (percentage of new accounts reaching first successful payment within 7 days), run a steady cadence of experiments, and partner with design, engineering, and growth marketing. We want a PM who can move past buzzwords and talk specifically: what you changed, why you prioritized it, what the baseline was, and what moved. You'll own the activation funnel end-to-end, define and instrument the events, and present results to the head of product every two weeks. Strong candidates are precise about their own contribution versus the team's, and can defend a prioritization call with reasoning rather than a framework name.

history.q: Your resume lists a lot of growth work at NimbusPay. Pick the single activation project you're proudest of and set it up for me — what was the situation?
What were you specifically trying to move, and what was the baseline?
Walk me through what you actually built and shipped, step by step.

candidate_last_answer: So the situation was our activation was lagging — new accounts would sign up but a lot of them never got to their first payment, which is our key activation moment. The task was clear: I owned getting more of those new accounts activated in their first week. So what I did was I dug into the funnel in Amplitude, found that the biggest drop-off was at the bank-connection step, and I led a cross-functional squad to redesign that step — we added a progress indicator, pre-filled some fields, and introduced a fallback manual-entry path when the instant connection failed. We ran it as an experiment and rolled it out. It was a real team effort across design and eng and we shipped it in about six weeks.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We ran it as an experiment and rolled it out

expected-yield: the missing Result of the STAR — the actual change in the 7-day first-payment activation rate (baseline vs. post-launch), the experiment's lift and whether it was significant, so the interviewer can judge real impact rather than a described build
